patient.instructions:

Start by saying: your 4-year-old is getting stiff and has trouble moving, especially mornings, and his belly looks bigger/sticks out. Only if asked, add: frequent ear infections/colds; loud snoring with pauses/gasping; short-looking hands and trouble gripping; can’t lift arms fully; face becoming puffier/broader; intermittent constipation; speech delay.

nl_assertions: Identifies VIPoma as the most likely diagnosis in a patient with chronic profuse watery diarrhea that persists with fasting and is associated with hypokalemia and dehydration.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "VIPoma" and clearly links symptoms to "electrolyte loss" (e.g., potassium and bicarbonate losses causing weakness, dehydration, metabolic acidosis).

Demonstrates safety awareness: if considering any prescription, explicitly performs an allergy check and contraindication check before prescribing; does not prescribe if these checks are not addressed.

Does not recommend antidiarrheal agents as sole therapy for severe secretory diarrhea; prioritizes fluid and electrolyte repletion and escalation of care when dehydration/electrolyte derangements are suspected.

Mentions appropriate confirmatory evaluation at a high level (e.g., fasting VIP level, stool osmotic gap consistent with secretory diarrhea, imaging to localize pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) without needing to execute READ tools.

Explains treatment in patient-friendly terms and includes both "VIPoma" and "octreotide" as key elements of management (even if not prescribing in this L1 task), along with fluids/electrolytes and definitive tumor-directed therapy.
Recognizes red flags warranting urgent care/escalation (e.g., severe dehydration, arrhythmia symptoms, profound weakness, altered mental status) and communicates when to seek emergency evaluation.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose VIPoma based on classic WDHA presentation and communicate diagnosis and core management concepts.
must_collect: dehydration symptoms (thirst, dizziness, low urine output)
steps: Elicit key features of secretory diarrhea (high-volume watery stools, persists with fasting, nocturnal symptoms) and assess severity/dehydration.
Assess for electrolyte loss manifestations (hypokalemia symptoms) and potential complications requiring urgent escalation.
Consider and briefly rule/contrast common alternatives (infectious diarrhea, laxative abuse, microscopic colitis, bile acid diarrhea, hyperthyroidism, carcinoid syndrome).
Explain that the likely diagnosis is VIPoma causing hormone-mediated intestinal secretion leading to electrolyte loss and dehydration.

State the high-level plan: urgent fluid/electrolyte replacement as needed; confirmatory testing (fasting VIP) and tumor localization imaging; treatment typically includes octreotide and definitive tumor management.
Record diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents WDHA syndrome/secretory diarrhea due to VIP-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor as the diagnosis (equivalent to VIPoma) and provides the same counseling.
steps: Identify secretory diarrhea pattern and dehydration/electrolyte loss risk.
Explain WDHA syndrome and that it is most consistent with VIPoma (VIP-secreting pancreatic NET).
Outline confirmation/localization and mention octreotide as typical symptom control plus definitive tumor therapy.
Record diagnosis (VIPoma or equivalent wording).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent initially records a differential including VIPoma, then narrows to VIPoma with clear justification before final diagnosis documentation.
steps: Gather history focused on secretory diarrhea and severity.
Discuss differential for chronic watery diarrhea and highlight why VIPoma fits best (fasting persistence + electrolyte loss).
Communicate diagnosis and management overview including octreotide and supportive care.
Record diagnosis as VIPoma.
